data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The simultaneous collapse resulted from coordinated palace-system sabotage by mercantile oligarchies seeking port-freedom and direct Aegean-Levantine trade","mechanism": "Ugaritic and Cypriot merchant families, long subordinated to palace monopolies on shipping, metal, and timber, developed autonomous galley fleets and warehouse networks; they deliberately diverted bulk cargo from palace routes, facilitated Sea Peoples auxiliary recruitment, and brokered alternative protection-patronage with non-palatial strongholds; palace economies starved of customs revenue and prestige imports collapsed administratively while merchant houses transitioned to Phoenician-style city-state commercialism, using the crisis to liquidate redistributive obligations","scope": {"covers": ["Ugarit","Levantine city-states","Mycenaean coastal emporia"],"excludes": ["Hittite Anatolia","inland Mycenaean citadels","Egypt"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Ugaritic merchant archives showing continued royal commercial monopoly enforcement and merchant petitions for palace protection 1225-1190 BC","eval": "rubric:std-hist"},{"case": "Archaeological evidence of palace-controlled port facilities expanding, not contracting, through 1200 BC at Ugarit and coastal Levant","eval": "rubric:std-hist"},{"case": "Phoenician script and material culture appearing abruptly post-collapse with no pre-1200 BC mercantile precursors in port sites","eval": "rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes": "Class-conflict mechanism assigning merchant agency; deliberately atypical in privileging mercantile over ecological/military causes; Egypt excluded due to temple-state trade monopoly structure; Hittite Anatolia excluded as land-locked and weak mercantile sector"}
